cards.text: To declare a static local variable, use the {{c1::static}} keyword inside a function: {{c2::static int count{ 0 };}}

cards.back: A local variable with static duration - it is created and initialized the first time the function is called and persists until the program ends.

cards.question: What does this function print when called 3 times?
cards.explanation: Static local variables retain their value between function calls. The variable is initialized only once and persists for the program's lifetime.

cards.front: When is a static local variable initialized?
cards.back: The first time the function is called and execution reaches the variable's definition. It is not re-initialized on subsequent calls.

cards.front: What is the scope of a static local variable?
cards.back: Block scope (just like normal local variables). It can only be accessed within the block where it's defined, even though it has static duration.

cards.front: What is the duration of a static local variable?
cards.back: Static duration. The variable is created when first initialized and destroyed when the program ends, not when the function exits.

cards.front: What value does a static local variable have if not explicitly initialized?
cards.back: Zero-initialized (just like global variables). This is different from normal local variables which are uninitialized.

cards.front: What is a common use case for static local variables?
cards.back: Generating unique IDs - the static variable maintains a counter that increments each time the function is called, ensuring each ID is unique.

cards.question: Complete this function to generate unique IDs:
cards.explanation: Use a static local variable to maintain state between calls. Each call increments and returns the next ID.

cards.front: Why are static local variables better than global variables for maintaining state?
cards.back: They have limited scope (only accessible within the function), making them safer and preventing unintended modification from other parts of the program.

cards.front: What happens if two static local variables in different functions have the same name?
cards.back: No problem. They are completely independent because each has block scope limited to its own function. The name doesn't conflict.

cards.concept: When to use static local variables
cards.explanation:
Use static local variables when you need to:
  • Maintain state between function calls
  • Generate unique identifiers
  • Cache expensive computations
  • Count function invocations
They provide the persistence of globals with the safety of local scope.

cards.text: Static local variables have {{c1::block scope}} and {{c2::static duration}} - they persist until the {{c3::program ends}}.
A static local variable is initialized {{c1::the first time}} the function is called and {{c2::not re-initialized}} on subsequent calls.
Static local variables that are not explicitly initialized are {{c1::zero-initialized}}, unlike normal local variables which are {{c2::uninitialized}}.
Static local variables have {{c1::block scope}} but {{c2::static duration}}, combining local accessibility with persistent state.
